title: How to Convert Between Wealth and Income Tax
gist: Paul Graham gives a conversion framework between wealth and income tax: divide the wealth-tax rate by the risk-free rate of return, so at a 5% return a 1% wealth tax equals a 20% income tax. He argues most politicians don't grasp this, meaning a 'mere 1%' wealth tax would in the median US state produce the highest marginal tax rate in the world.

insights.claim: A wealth tax rate converts to an equivalent income tax rate by dividing by the rate of return on capital, so at an assumed 5% risk-free return a 1% wealth tax equals a 20% income tax.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Lets an investor instantly translate any proposed wealth tax into its far larger income-tax equivalent when assessing jurisdictional tax risk.
insights.quote: To convert between wealth and income tax rates, you have to divide by the rate of return on capital.
insights.locator: In fact the conversion rate between them is about 20

insights.claim: The correct denominator for the conversion is the risk-free rate of return because a wealth tax, considered as an anti-investment, is itself risk-free: the holder will certainly owe the money.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Clarifies why higher risky returns should not be used to soften a wealth tax's apparent burden; the liability is certain.
insights.quote: considered as an anti-investment, a wealth tax is absolutely risk-free: you will absolutely owe the government that money.

insights.claim: The same conversion multiple applies to capital gains, with the multiple's source being whether money is taxed every year versus just once, the identical math used to value any income-generating asset.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Extends the annual-versus-one-time-tax intuition to capital-gains policy and to valuing income-producing assets generally.
insights.quote: The same conversion rate applies to capital gains. The source of the multiple is whether the money is taxed every year or just once.

insights.claim: Politicians reveal they don't grasp the conversion by speaking of a 'mere 1%' wealth tax while never proposing a 'mere 20%' income tax increase, even though the two are mathematically equivalent.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Signals that wealth-tax proposals may be systematically underestimated by their proposers, a source of underpriced tax-policy risk for asset holders.
insights.quote: None of them would speak of adding a "mere 20%" to the income tax rate, even though that's mathematically the same thing.
insights.locator: It's clear that politicians don't get this

insights.claim: In the median US state a 1% wealth tax would push the total marginal rate to roughly 61.75% (37% federal + 4.75% state + 20% equivalent), exceeding Denmark's world-high 60.5% income tax rate.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Quantifies that a seemingly small wealth tax can make a US state's effective tax burden the highest on earth, a concrete relocation/positioning trigger for the wealthy.
insights.quote: a state adding an additional 20% in income tax would have a total marginal tax rate of 37% + 4.75% + 20%, or 61.75%.
insights.locator: Currently the country with the highest marginal income tax rate is Denmark
